evidence: Action contract data is validated across multiple registries in tests: uploadsvc compares `contracts.UploadActionBindingSpecs()`, `UploadActionContractByAction()`, and `contracts.ActionContractFor(...)` in `goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go`.
Conformance tests separately verify fixture bindings against canonical bindings while also referencing familycatalog constants in `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go`.

The contract catalog embeds concrete owner module path strings (`uploadsvc`, `system/wifi`) in `goseg/protocol/contracts/familycatalog/catalog.go`, coupling protocol contracts to implementation package layout.

findings.summary: Upload action contract truth is split across catalog, bindings, and service mappings.
evidence: `actions_test.go` must cross-check three APIs (`UploadActionBindingSpecs`, `UploadActionContractByAction`, `ActionContractFor`) to confirm consistency, showing duplicated contract ownership.
`fixtures_test.go` revalidates binding parity against canonical contract bindings, indicating additional synchronization surface.
`catalog.go` defines the same action domain metadata (namespace/action/contract/name/description), creating another contract-definition layer.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate upload action contract definition into one canonical registry (preferably protocol contracts/family catalog) and have uploadsvc consume typed descriptors directly; remove parallel mapping surfaces so tests verify behavior rather than cross-registry parity.

findings.summary: Protocol contract catalog is coupled to concrete runtime package paths via owner strings.
evidence: `catalog.go` hard-codes owner values as implementation-oriented paths (`OwnerUploadService = "uploadsvc"`, `OwnerSystemWiFi = "system/wifi"`).

`doc.go` defines upload action validation semantics inside uploadsvc while catalog also carries action metadata/ownership, splitting boundary responsibility between protocol and runtime package details.
findings.suggestion: Replace path-like owner strings with domain-stable ownership identifiers (or remove owner from protocol specs) and keep implementation/package mapping in a runtime composition layer so protocol contracts remain independent of package structure.

findings.summary: Governance policy is documented as a cross-module contract, but the executable checks only cover action-binding metadata and miss documented payload-contract rules.
evidence: README.md states a governance gate must keep contract fixtures and family-governance checks green.
uploadsvc/doc.go defines strict payload rules (`reset` must not carry open-endpoint payload fields; `open-endpoint` requires complete endpoint/token payload).
fixtures_test.go validates fixture namespace/action/contract relationships only.
actions_test.go validates action parsing and descriptor metadata, but not payload-shape conformance.
findings.suggestion: Promote upload payload rules into shared contract fixtures/specs (schema-level fields per action) and enforce them in conformance tests so README governance policy maps directly to runtime-checked contracts.

findings.summary: Fixture parity tests enforce lockstep module updates, leaving no compatibility/deprecation lane for contract evolution.
evidence: fixtures_test.go fails when fixture and canonical binding counts differ and rejects any extra fixture key.
catalog.go action specs expose namespace/action/contract/name/description/owner but no version/deprecation/alias metadata for phased migration.
README.md frames these checks as governance gates, so this strict equality becomes the architectural evolution policy in practice.
findings.suggestion: Add explicit compatibility metadata (e.g., version, status=active/deprecated, replacement contract) in contract specs and update conformance tests to validate governed migration states instead of only exact one-to-one parity.

findings.summary: Consumer tests reveal two authorities for upload action behavior (consumer parser/actions and protocol registry), increasing boundary coordination overhead.
evidence: actions_test.go has to reconcile `ParseUploadAction`/`SupportedUploadActions` results with `contracts.UploadActionBindingSpecs()` and `contracts.ActionContractFor(...)`.
catalog.go defines canonical action specs, but consumer-side action acceptance is validated through cross-check tests instead of deriving parser capability directly from the catalog boundary.
README.md assigns shared ownership across protocol/contracts and uploadsvc, matching this duplicated coordination surface.
findings.suggestion: Refactor upload action parsing/dispatch to derive accepted action set and metadata from the protocol contract catalog directly (single source of truth), keeping consumer code as adapter logic rather than parallel action authority.
